item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article has been replaced with a cc-compliant alternative. The components used in this image come from here and here, and are cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The footnotes should be mouse-over-able/clickable. On mobile its a little tricky and it might be easier to just read the page source. The "link" to MS-2342 is fake, however.

On the other hand How I Found Goddess (and What I Did To Her When I Found Her).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like where you're going at but I don't know how to click on the links. Or am I not supposed to do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
whaaaaaaat have I just found?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, it's a bastard to read.

So I just went and read it directly from the page source. Missing the point? Yep. But it's much easier to understand.

+1? I think that's what I rated it. Can't remember.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad that at least one page on this site exists to point out the fifthist-discordian connection. ^^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh, the SCP appears to have been built around the format screw, not the other way around, which in most cases, doesn't really work that well.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the SCP equivalent of coming up with the title of a movie first and working backwards. Only instead of writing a movie around a title, we are writing a skip to justify a format screw. Which would work if it weren't so obvious.

Meta in service to an interesting idea = acceptable if done well.

Bending over backwards to stretch an idea to justify a novel meta SCP, not so much. But honestly, this is a growing trend on the site in general so I can't fault this entry alone.,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not actually reading an afterimage of an article that has been memetically unpacked from an image.

Thankfully, we're also not inserting images with fractals in them that act as memetic killagents, and that an MTF team isn't breaking into our home after reading 2718.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like pretty much all of what's going on here, and I'll happily +1 for the actual effects. What really gets me, though, is that I'm instinctively trying to figure out how the original French in the addendum would sound, and I can't come up with anything that wouldn't be aggressively Joual. Somehow that seems appropriate for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Zed's dead, baby

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have two reasonably interesting ideas — the religion in question, and its anomalous effect (including the subsequent format screw) — that are only tied together by an incredibly tenuous thread. It's not thematically coherent and just barely narratively coherent, and I'm left wondering why they're in the same article. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not actually reading an afterimage of an article that has been memetically unpacked from an image. I'm reading an article that is entirely composed out of footnotes and annoying as shit to read, then have to go to the page source to actually get the link for the document to find out that it doesn't exist and the somewhat interesting effect was entirely invented to justify writing an article composed of footnotes and there's completely nothing else in here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Praise Eris!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Zed's a Fifthist!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit I knew nothing of Discordianism before reading this, but the lackadaisical tone of the addendum is refreshing when compared to all of the dreary religious stuff I've read over the years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fithing this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept and format. On the other hand, if the author makes MS-2342 and MS-2342-ARC in a red font, please make them accessible working links.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed with others. This takes an interesting concept and rather than exploring it, twists it into "how can we use this concept to justify a very screwy format screw". It's the opposite of an organic narrative. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite it being extremely difficult to read on mobile, I had to reload the page a couple of times, I still really like the format and the concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fourthing this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddammit

god

DAMMIT

I am so mad because I've had a "footnotes only" skip in the works forever and now everyone's doing footnotes and I'm just going to come off as a copycat because this is even better than what I was trying to do and I hate you ._. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirding this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having read it (on my phone, by reading page source), the idea of people able to detect perception is fairly neat. The Discordian parts are amusing, but trying to make me take them seriously for the sake of the narrative isn't. I wasn't able to navigate to the crosslinks for depth. The containment procedures aren't actually containment procedures, and I'm assuming Red Herring just had to make things up as they go along, no writing.

If it were just content, I'd probably be neutral leaning towards upvote.

All of that said, screw this format screw. I understand what you were trying to accomplish and why you chose the method you did, but I didn't perceive this as info spontaneously appearing. Maybe if this were five years into the future and every computer had an eyetracker, and you could make text spontaneously appear as you look, that would get the idea across. This just feels like extensive noodling with wikidot's limited syntax making this hard to read.

Edit: I've gone back and read it again on a desktop. It was less annoying (it could hardly be more so), and I appreciate the incorporation of the crosslinks (although, again, not being able to click them is annoying), but ultimately the annoyance still outweighed the low extent to which it was thematic.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: in footnote 10 there's an SCP-2342-3 that should be a -2, unless they excerpted the text from a person D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to downvote this because of the screw,
I really did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect there's only a handful of people who have worn dog tags bearing "Discordian" into combat

I would 100% re-enlist solely to do this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. That really feels more contrived and weak than I originally thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love Goddess as much the next Sir Giuseppe Pudding, Knight Commander of The Order of Captain Starfish of The Spoon Inflicted Stigmata, Last Keeper of the Sacred Egg of Yog, Destroyer of the Sacred Egg of Yog, Pourer of the Syrup of the Lucid Pancakes, Pope of the Paratheo-anametamystikhood Of Eris Esoteric (I suspect there's only a handful of people who have worn dog tags bearing "Discordian" into combat1, I am wearing a Sacred Chao as I type, and I have twice made the pilgrimage to St. Joshua's grave) but including Her in the Foundation-verse pretty much straight seems like a direct inclusion of a pop culture thing.

ETA: Also if fnord stress sigils existed in the Foundation-verse, they would already be a big part of the Foundation's subliminal memetics toolkit.

ETA2: Saturn Deer already fills the same role that Eris does in this setting, making Goddess redundant at best here.

Footnotes
1. And here I was probably risking my life for my faith, in that Muslims might interpret it as apostacy or atheism (although "No Preference" wouldn't have been much safer).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit that I find the Discordian shtick tiresome, which probably affects my vote somewhat. But the footnote thing feels contrived as hell. "Probably memetic or something" isn't selling it all that well to me either.

What's unfortunate is that the basic idea of something where the person who created it has awareness of perception of the thing is an interesting starting point for something. The reader-author mind meld being a two way relationship has some interesting implications. But that isn't really explored beyond "hey, let's make this ever-so-wacky religion more popular, lol Fifthists". So I guess I disagree fundamentally that the "how do we keep people from knowing it's being read" angle is what should be front and center. The problem with format screws is usually that it's smoke and mirrors trying to hide the fact that nothing's there. Here it's the opposite problem; the formatting is obscuring the potential interest of the article.

The fact that the conversation is mostly focused on the format screw may have been what you were going for, but to me the formatting is in service to the article content, not the other way around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent format screw. Love the idea of the discordants' version of a religious experience involving waking up in France after a bender with a bunch of strangers and a mildly incoherent new holy text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha, I did the same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was slightly let down that there was no actual link to MS-2342 or page that exists after digging through the source to find it.

Still, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hail Eris!

…and this Jaime guy too, I guess…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally, Eris herself had written a note on the back, then signed it. "What's in this book is 100% true. Make sure you get me really popular this time around - don't worry, you'll be able to tell."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not seeing the link between the religion and having the ability to know when anyone is reading about them. The format screw is justified, but it doesn't feel cohesive except maybe for some grand leaps of association, like chaos -> destruction -> removal of barriers of information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote. The concept and execution are intriguing and well-done, but it was difficult to read through all the footnotes and it's almost unreadable on mobile devices. This is an interesting format screw that hasn't quite overcome its technical challenges.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saw the explanation, like it enough to upvote.

And also am I reading a scip that has been uploaded for just a few hours?

Edit: saw a small 1 at where the title is supposed to be at. Is that supposed to be like a footnote thingy? Cos nothing happens when I pressed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OK now this makes sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddammit Rando.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What in the name of jeebus is this!

This has got to be the most distorted meta SCP I have ever read that isn't hard to read!

(unless you are on mobile)

I will rate when I read it but good job on the idea of the meta SCP style! You SCP people just get better and better at your craft!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I now have brain pain, guys. Is that normal?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TLDR: The hallucinations are causing you to hallucinate.

There are no footnotes on this page. The page is, in fact, entirely devoid of data, bar the image and standard data anchors - the titles in bold for each section. The image on the page produced a memetic effect to cause you to believe that there were footnotes which contained the information that you read.

If the image on the page caused you to directly hallucinate plaintext on the page, then your perception of that image is interpreting written information about an SCP-2342-3 entity; the written information is being unpacked from that image, and as such, the image is a direct encoding of written data, and an SCP-2342-3 entity who is aware of how this works could detect your observation of that.

Instead, the image introduces hallucinatory footnotes adjacent to the other data anchor points. These serve to cause further memetic effects, such that you are able to understand the words that "appear" - or rather, that you are hallucinating to appear - when you move your mouse over them. Reading these, however, is an interpretation of data encoded in the footnotes as you have hallucinated them, which do not exist external to your own mind. As such, SCP-2342-3 instances are unable to detect this.

Think of it like the memetic version of a visual aftereffect: by the time your brain interprets the data as meaningful, you aren't interpreting it from the original source, but your memory of it; the unpacking is delayed. As such, when you're actually interpreting the data as meaningful, you're not viewing it directly, so they can't detect it.

Double-delayed memetic separation. Hail Eris!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Knee-jerked downvote, then actually turned my brain on.

I like it!

Nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might be missing something, but why footnotes (of all ways to conceal data)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haven't voted yet, but I can tell you reading this on a phone is a significant pain in the ass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. Well done, clever execution, but this is very hard to read and clumsy, I know it's the part of the SCP itself but I'm generally not a fan of these kinds of things. I literally re-read the whole thing by looking at the script. The containment procedures remind me a bit of SCP-2521.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Long in the works as a challenge from Deci, let's see if it works for long!
